title: The same bug shows up in almost every LLM-written backtest we've looked at
author: Nvestiq
selftext:
We've spent the last few months looking at trading code people generated with ChatGPT, Claude, and Cursor. Strategies in Pine Script, Python, MQL5, you name it. There's one bug that shows up over and over, and the equity curves never look broken because of it.

The model writes signal logic that references the current bar's close to decide whether to enter on that same bar. In live trading, you can't act on a close before it's happened. In a backtest with sloppy indexing, you can, and the strategy looks brilliant. Look-ahead bias, baked in.

LLMs do this constantly because the training data is full of educational code where indicator-on-close gets computed across the full series without thinking about real-time causality. The model isn't reasoning about when information becomes available; it's pattern-matching to "compute SMA, compare to close, generate signal."

You can't really catch it by reading the output either. The Python looks clean. The Pine Script compiles. The Sharpe is 2.4 and the curve goes up and to the right. The first time you find it is usually when live PnL doesn't match week one.

Full disclosure, we work on backtesting infrastructure, so this is the problem I think about constantly. We're interested in what others have found when you've used an LLM to write strategy code. Has lookahead been the bug that bit you, or have you seen worse?

comments.author: d_e_g_m
comments.body: I noticed this as well, and it was natural since my initial backteat data was 1 minute aggregated. That is why I modified my backtesting strategy to wait for close bars and enter the next and my strategy also waits for 1 minute close bars.

replies.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
replies.body: I also had to tinker with the sleep/wait commands that are used to stop the one minute candle traders from hammering servers.  If it waits 55 seconds to grab next candle, you have a weird float that cycles through the day.  It needs to open a channel to the api and say report back when candle close and I’ll wait for response

replies.author: Much_Bullfrog_2631
replies.body: Websockets are the way to go here. I use topsteps websocket to get tick by tick data feeding continuously with a failsafe if the websocket connection drops as well as a terminal print every 15 seconds just to make sure we getting prices coming in

replies.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
replies.body: A lot of people who don’t “engineer the waiting” are just making a denial of service bot.

replies.author: Much_Bullfrog_2631
replies.body: Nah websockets are meant to give you continuous data streams unlike a API request that requires calls to the endpoint each time

replies.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
replies.body: Sorry, I thought i was agreeing with you. Words hard today.

replies.author: Much_Bullfrog_2631
replies.body: Lmao my bad reading hard today too lol

comments.author: Minimum_Raccoon_1501
comments.body: Had to fix this yesterday in my system.  You have to expressly tell the llm coding agent to make sure it isn’t leaking temporal order when back testing.  It will usually understand but still need to directly say make sure that today’s trades are based on past information like yesterday close.

comments.author: Much_Bullfrog_2631
comments.body: Ive dealt with lookahead bias, the best way to avoid it is to created dedicated out of sample testing. What i do is break up my historical dataset 80/20 split chronologically. 80% is used to build your strategy and the 20% is for testing. Helps a lot but if your using LLM just be sure the candle information in the files arent using any sort of future indicator. For example I had a column that was tracking the days high but it was later in the day so earlier candles could not have known that

comments.author: climbcolorado
comments.body: Yup, 100% got fooled by some of the first backtest results.

comments.author: debdebweb
comments.body:
OMG this is the problem I have been battling with AI. Claude. ChatGPT. Gemini. It’s like pulling teeth to get them to understand entering the next candle at the close price of the previous one.  I couldn’t figure out why this was so freaking hard for it to do!

And another issue is getting it to respect a stop limit mid-candle. It seems to be willing close at the open or close but has some weird issue with anywhere in the middle. 

I finally got Claude and Chat to do it but it took superhuman tenacity to get there. 

I thought that I must be asking it to do something really unusual.

comments.author: Either_Door_5500
comments.body:
You hit the nail on the head and this is a massive issue with LLM-generated quant code. LLMs naturally try to write clean, logical code, but they completely lack context for the temporal nature of financial data. The second area they often leak in is they assume a data point labeled for Q1 was available on the last day of Q1, completely ignoring the forty day filing lag and subsequent corporate amendments.

Fixing this at the code or prompt level is almost impossible because the LLM is just working with the flat database array it is given. The only way to truly eliminate this bug is at the data layer itself by using a point-in-time architecture that physically prevents the execution engine from seeing data that was not legally public at that exact time. If your data feed does not support temporal rollbacks, your backtest is going to cheat every single time.

Feel free to dm if you want to go deeper. an api I built that provides backtest-friendly SEC data with amendment trails for devs - this is what I do day-to-day.

comments.author: Local-March-7400
comments.body: all of you guys in this sub have no fkn idea of fundamentals. Please just learn about biases in trading like look ahead, same day execution or survivorship bias, Market regime and learn to understand software engineering and code you can even ask AI for help. As someone whos been working 2 years on my strategyidea, backtestengine and now Trading program, without proper understanding of Computer science the chance of blowing up over time is almost 100 percent. This field is the hardest to make easy money... if you try to make strategy code with AI you have to validate it and need to know what questions to ask
